summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he stopped self-hosting his WordPress blog and moved to WPEngine's managed hosting. After years of fighting Apache KeepAlive issues, WordPress security vulnerabilities, server crashes during traffic spikes, and routine maintenance that consumed billable weeks of his time, he was convinced by WPEngine founder Jason Cohen's technical expertise and the service's superior infrastructure. The migration resulted in faster page loads, zero downtime during high-traffic events, and freed him to focus on his actual businesses instead of server administration.
key_points: Apache's KeepAlive setting was the root cause of his blog crashing under modest traffic loads (10K-100K pageviews), and turning it off was the single biggest stability improvement
Self-hosting WordPress consumed billable weeks of time on security hardening, patching, backups, and infrastructure maintenance that produced zero business value
WPEngine's architecture (load balancer → Varnish → Nginx → Apache) was so superior that it actually reversed his KeepAlive advice — in a properly architected stack, you want KeepAlive on
The $2,500/year hosting cost was trivial compared to the opportunity cost of time spent on server administration instead of running businesses
Getting technically corrected and re-educated by Jason Cohen on infrastructure architecture was the pivotal moment that changed his mind
money_quotes: I've probably lost in excess of 100,000 readers over the years for the blog being down.
If it weren't the best blogging software available I'd take a hammer to my backups then burn the shards and bury them on sanctified ground so that they never troubled the world again.
You know how often I talk to software company CEOs and get not just corrected but destroyed and then re-educated about a point of technical fact? Suffice it to say it made an impression.
Hosting WordPress has been a black hole of my time.
$2,500 a year for blog hosting sounds a bit on the high side, but it is honestly nothing against the amount of time that I will no longer have to invest supporting this sucker.
I love being out of the hosting business. I never intended on being in it in the first place — it was always just something I needed to do to write for people.
key_insight: The opportunity cost of self-hosting infrastructure you don't need to control vastly exceeds the dollar cost of paying someone who has solved the problem properly.
